case_name: Wilcox v  King's College Hospital NHS Foundation Trust [2020] EWHC 2555 (QB) (09 July 2020)

evidence_index.text: The claim arises from an admitted delay in the diagnosis and treatment of the Claimant's cauda equina compression in August 2016.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Claimant has sustained a permanent disability in the form of an incomplete paraplegia at L3 with reduced lower limb strength and sensation and neuropathic pain affecting both lower limbs.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A report on condition and prognosis from Mr Gawronski, annexed to the Particulars of Claim, substantiates the Claimant's complaints.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: Judgment was entered on the basis of the admission that there had been a culpable delay in treating the Claimant's symptoms.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Claimant's witness statement of October 2019 which refers to the impact of Claimant's disabilities on his activities of daily living.
evidence_index.speaker: Claimant

evidence_index.text: The Deputy Master concluded that there was no reason to impugn the work undertaken by Robertson and Co nor the selection of the footage included in the clip which the Defendant proposed to deploy.
evidence_index.speaker: Deputy Master

evidence_index.text: The Deputy Master found that there were really no significant inconsistencies between what the Claimant was shown to be doing on the footage and what he had said concerning his disabilities.
evidence_index.speaker: Deputy Master

evidence_index.text: I dismiss the appeal for two reasons.
evidence_index.speaker: Mrs Justice Lambert

evidence_index.text: The video evidence is not admissible simply because it is not relevant.
evidence_index.speaker: Mrs Justice Lambert

initial_state.chief_complaint: Symptoms suggestive of cauda equina compression

requestables.name: MRI Spine
requestables.description: Imaging to confirm cauda equina compression

reveal.clinical_significance: Essential for diagnosing cauda equina compression

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with symptoms suggestive of cauda equina compression

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with suspected cauda equina compression
decision_points.prompt: What is the appropriate imaging to confirm diagnosis?

options.clinical_reasoning: MRI is the standard imaging for diagnosing cauda equina compression
Delay in imaging can lead to worsening of condition

expected_action_court.description: MRI should have been ordered promptly
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established MRI is standard

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Delay in imaging led to delay in treatment
explanation.what_should_have_happened: MRI should have been performed urgently

patient_outcome.description: Patient sustained permanent disability due to delayed treatment
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Incomplete paraplegia at L3, reduced lower limb strength and sensation, neuropathic pain

key_findings: Breach of duty in delaying diagnosis and treatment
Causation established - earlier intervention would have improved outcome

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to promptly order MRI and treat cauda equina compression
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Timely MRI and surgery would have prevented or reduced disability

factual_timeline.event: Delay in diagnosis and treatment of cauda equina compression
